props.covers: The complete connector slice at packages/canvas/src/connectors/: the def (connectors are not objects), elbow routing and anchor resolution, waypoint reconciliation, bend editing, the connection cascade, creation/reconnect gestures and the Connector Mode tool, the gesture/overlay vocabulary, the MPL pathfinding island, and the four render components.

text.insert: The connector slice runs from def to route math to gestures to pixels in one folder:

owns the non-object connector def, elbow routing and anchor resolution, waypoint reconciliation helpers, bend editing, the connection cascade, gesture steppers, the pathfinding island, overlays, and render components. A connection's visual path is never stored as a fixed drawing — it is computed from the current geometry of its two endpoint objects every time either side moves.
is the single object-to-object routing entry point: given a from-object, a to-object, and a connection's anchor/position/waypoint overrides, it returns a

— an SVG path string, the true start/end anchor points, a label placement point, and the route's world-space vertex list. The router is style-agnostic: solid and dashed connectors share the same route, and there are no straight/smooth branches here. Its branches are explicit orthogonal
, near-straight exact anchoring, simple elbow routing, and an obstacle-triggered orthogonal A* fallback.
first resolves bounds, anchors, explicit side overrides, and explicit relative
exist and form an orthogonal polyline,
uses them. Otherwise the router tries an exact straight route when facing anchors are already aligned, then builds a simple
path. Only when that simple elbow crosses an extra obstacle does it call

— an A* pathfinder adapted from BlockSuite (an MPL-2.0 licensed open-source project). If A* throws, returns fewer than two usable points, or otherwise yields no route, the caller returns the already-computed simple elbow.
The A* algorithm itself lives under
are algorithmically verbatim ports (import paths adjusted only), and
is verbatim with import specifiers rewritten to point at the local
is explicitly not verbatim — it's a labeled extraction of only the pure, framework-free orthogonal path-generation logic (
and its supporting helpers) from BlockSuite's
/model, and canvas-paint/overlay code stripped out, and no rotation support (this repo's object schema has no per-object rotation field).
has no direct upstream file counterpart — it's a dependency-free reimplementation of the small set of

primitives (IVec, Bound, Vec, PointLocation, line/polygon intersection helpers) the other BlockSuite-derived files need, preserving the same math and semantics without pulling in the upstream package.
Whichever branch produces the route, waypoints are deduplicated (
) and turned into a rendered SVG path by

, which rounds interior corners (clamped to half the shorter adjacent segment's length, so short segments still get a sane smaller radius rather than overshooting) and pulls the first/last rendered points back by a fixed end-gap along their own tangent so the drawn stroke never touches the target border — the connection's
fields still carry the true, untouched anchor points for endpoint-drag handles and label placement.
decides which side of each endpoint object the connection attaches to.

now prefers genuinely facing sides before falling back to center-delta dominance: if the objects' vertical spans overlap within the 8px auto-route span-overlap tolerance, it chooses right/left or left/right; if their horizontal spans overlap, it chooses bottom/top or top/bottom; otherwise the dominant center separation wins, with horizontal ties going horizontal. This 8px value is
in the router, not the cascade's screen-pixel snap radius. This picked pair is only the fallback: an explicit
values) wins when set, and an explicit relative
fraction of an object's bounds, resolved by
) overrides even the anchor side entirely, landing the endpoint at a specific point rather than a side midpoint.
The routing module also exposes two lower-level anchor helpers used elsewhere in the engine:
returns all four side-midpoints of a bounds, and

picks whichever of those four is closest to an arbitrary point — a plain bounding-box heuristic, distinct from the real-outline anchor projection objects/geometry.ts performs for live drag/hover interaction (see below).
) are stored as absolute world coordinates, so nothing in
itself keeps them coherent when an endpoint object moves — that reconciliation happens one layer up, in the action reducer.

— a choke point wrapped around every document-producing action (drag/section-carry commits, nudges, resize, align/distribute, fit-section, inspector geometry patches) — so the waypoint fix-up shares the action's own history entry and a single undo restores both geometry and waypoints together.
are exempt since they restore stored document snapshots verbatim.
The reconciliation is asymmetric by design, per connection. For each waypointed connection, it compares its two endpoint owners' geometry before and after the action: if

delta and neither resized — e.g. both riding a carried section, a multi-select drag, or a nudge — the waypoints translate rigidly by that same delta, preserving the connection's trunk-and-branch fan shape exactly. If only one owner moved, the two owners moved by different deltas, or either owner resized, the waypoints are dropped (

) entirely so the connector falls back to auto-routing on next render rather than sweeping stale waypoints through space that's no longer meaningful relative to the shape — matching FigJam's re-route-on-asymmetric-move behavior. Duplicate and clipboard-paste actions handle the newly-created-object case separately, translating cloned waypoints by the paste offset at creation time rather than through this before/after comparison.

This rigid-translate/asymmetric-re-route split also applies to live-preview (non-history) drag frames, not just committed drags, so a fan of waypointed connectors visibly tracks a section as it's being dragged rather than only snapping into place on drop.

is a separate, purely-computational module for the live interaction surface around making and re-pointing connections — it is not the routing algorithm itself, but the logic that decides where a connector endpoint should snap while a person is dragging it. Since the P3 geometry move (OBJECT-DEF-OVERHAUL.md (retired; see git history), D4) it carries exactly ONE port from BlockSuite's
's decision cascade). The outline polygons and 4-cardinal anchor projection the cascade consumes moved into
— the defs own their outline geometry now — and the connector boundary is broader than the old single-edge claim:
, with only the current stage-core exceptions pinned to
. The tight pin is the MPL island:

imports no first-party code outside itself. The port remains algorithm-only: no canvas-paint, no GfxController/grid search, no rotation support. Its current behavior also deliberately excludes section interiors from the inside snap tier while keeping section anchors and outlines valid.
) computes 4 cardinal port candidates (N/S/E/W of an object's center, offset
outside the bound) and projects each onto the object's real outline — not its bounding box — via
and a center-to-candidate line/outline intersection. Which outline that is comes from the def-declared

: true (non-bounding-box) outlines cover pill/stadium, diamond, arrow-shape, and the W5 parity set (ellipse, triangle, parallelogram, pentagon, octagon, star, plus, chevron, off-page-connector, trapezoid, manual-input, hexagon, and the or/summing junction circles); every other object type falls back to its axis-aligned bounding rect, matching upstream's own conservative fallback. The same anchors also feed the selected-object anchor dots (
) and hit-testing (D16), so attachment, affordance, and clicks can never disagree.
The cascade is intentionally tiered and ordered. It iterates supplied

and immediately returning the first candidate that wins. For that candidate it requires the pointer to be inside the hover-expanded bounds; then it tries nearest cardinal anchor strictly within the screen-pixel snap radius, nearest outline point strictly within the world-pixel radius, and finally inside-shape resolution for eligible non-section shapes. If no candidate wins, it returns
. Section interiors are intentionally free canvas, but their anchors and outlines still participate in the higher snap tiers.

props.title: MPL-2.0 provenance is file-level and must stay demarcated

text.insert: is reimplemented, and each file keeps its MPL header. The full license/provenance story lives in
The connector slice keeps five in-slice tests under
covers auto anchors, exact-straight routing, style-agnostic routing, free-point previews, and endpoint gaps;
covers obstacle avoidance, determinism, 3-arg compatibility, clean no-obstacle elbow routes, explicit waypoint precedence, and endpoint
keeps the BlockSuite path generator deterministic;
covers the cascade layer while outline-polygon and anchor-projection geometry lives under
covers bend handles, drag snapping, simplification, and clear-only-when-straight commits. Related state and kernel coverage lives in
for rigid waypoint translation/drop behavior and
for Connector Mode behavior. The four render components have no dedicated in-slice component tests today; DOM coverage rides on the stage-level suites.
The net boundary is: routing computes world-space route
from endpoint geometry; rendering decides how to draw that path; interaction decides when a user-authored bend should become persistent

. That separation keeps automatic routing disposable while preserving explicitly edited orthogonal bends until a reconnect, asymmetric move, resize, or straightening simplification makes them invalid. The editor pipeline that dispatches these gestures is covered in
, and the kernel vocabulary underneath is covered in
The straightening path preserves the no-diagonals invariant. Stored waypoints are converted from tuples, deduped with the start/end points, and accepted only when every segment is orthogonal within
= 0.5px; diagonal waypoint edits are ignored and auto-routing resumes. A* output that is nearly straight is flattened through
: the anchor may slide along the object edge overlap span, trimmed by the connector end gap, so aligned objects get an exact two-point route instead of a tiny dogleg.

handles the free-point preview used while creating a connector toward empty canvas. It anchors from the source object, picks the free endpoint side by dominant delta, returns a straight two-point route when the pointer is exactly outward from the source anchor, and otherwise uses
. Preview routing intentionally avoids the A* obstacle pass, but it still uses the same rounded-corner and end-gap path construction as committed routes.

exposes every orthogonal connector segment as a draggable bend handle, including its segment index, midpoint, handle point, and horizontal/vertical axis. Non-orthogonal and degenerate segments are ignored; when a label lies on a segment, the handle point is shifted away from the label so the pill affordance remains reachable.

: horizontal segments move only by pointer dy, vertical segments only by pointer dx, and direct one-segment connectors expand into a stub-run-stub polyline instead of disappearing. During drag, same-orientation segment snaps and endpoint-straightening use
and immediately simplify the preview when a snap lands.
with the zoom-scaled tolerance. It normalizes nearly orthogonal segments, collapses tiny hooks and near duplicates, and merges collinear triples. Persistent
are cleared only when the simplified route is straight; otherwise the interior corners are stored. Endpoint reconnects also clear existing waypoints through
so old bends cannot survive after either endpoint changes.
The def: connectors are not objects
: connections are a separate document collection with
orders the toolbar controls as color, text, dash, and arrowhead; color, dash, and arrowhead are flyout-backed, while text opens the routed-midpoint label editor directly.
stamp new connectors with that gray pick. The old
set died in the P1 cutover.
The connector slice owns three gesture steppers in
. The editor pipeline dispatcher calls them from
resolves the hover target by running
reversed to topmost-first, excluding the other endpoint's object. It emits an explicit stored
only when the snapped anchor/outline point is not a plain bounding-box canonical anchor.
A dragged create release on a candidate object dispatches

, and a sub-threshold port click quick-connects from its pinned anchor while a sub-threshold body click is a no-op. The quick-connect path also seeds inline text editing for the newly duplicated object, and
places that ghost at an edge-to-edge gap of
from the source. Endpoint reconnect commits
and clears stored waypoints whenever the endpoint actually changed by object id, anchor, or position.
is a wiring mode, not a placeable object type:
includes it as the Connector Mode exception, and
exposes the dock entry with tooltip
is mode-specific: pointer-down on a port starts a
; pointer-down on an object body starts one without
; anything else stays idle. Double-click is a no-op in connector mode, and completing or canceling a connection does not switch the tool back because the connector-create paths never dispatch
so ports stay live at any zoom; select mode keeps the
are only for anchor/outline snaps, and
is absent when the create started from an object body.
are slice-owned and assembled into the editor's
with the gray fallback; dashed style uses
mounts it in a layer above the object layer so shape bodies never cover affordances; see
renders the dragged polyline during bend drags; otherwise it builds a routed preview through
in top/bottom/left/right order with the snapped anchor emphasized; outline snaps add a dot at the candidate point. Quick-connect preview adds a ghost clone of the source object rendered through
at world size; the preview connector stroke is gray at opacity 0.6, while the ghost object itself renders at opacity 0.35.
renders screen-space dots at bounding-box side midpoints offset by
= 0.5 gates select-mode visibility and interactivity, bypassed in Connector Mode; each dot is a DOM target with
= 28, and the stage hit resolution turns it into a
. This replaced the old invisible edge-port scheme.
There are two connector label components in the slice:
renders the at-rest inline SVG label group in
is a world-overlay pill positioned at the routed
but is not imported or mounted anywhere under
today. The current editor world overlay mounts
only for active inline editing, so the current editor/viewer composition does not render both label components for the same connection.
